reachability_fact: hotstuff_predict.iii:55-63 — the write loop `out_quorum[(i as u64)*32u64 + j] = ...` runs for `i` in `0..qsize` with no cap check. qsize can be up to `HSP_MAX_PEERS = 64` (line 23), yielding 2048 bytes written to a raw `*u8` that is only NULL-checked (line 47). The three live callers are all in `hsp_selftest` (lines 86, 92, 97); each passes a module-global buffer (`HSP_KAT_OUT`/`HSP_KAT_OUT2`, 256 bytes each). With n=7 the current KAT qsize=6 (192 bytes) fits in 256 and never fires; but with n=64 (f=21, qsize=min(44,64)=44) the write is 1408 bytes into a 256-byte slot — a real overwrite of adjacent module globals. No guard exists. No prior audit marker claims this is safe.
maximal_design: Thread `out_cap: u32` (byte capacity of the out_quorum buffer) as a 4th parameter to `hsp_predict_quorum`. Add `HSP_E_OVERFLOW : i32 = -3i32`. After qsize is clamped (line 52), check `qsize * 32u32 > out_cap` using overflow-safe form `qsize > out_cap / 32u32` (integer division; avoids multiply overflow; consistent with III subtraction-safe idiom), and return `HSP_E_OVERFLOW` before any write. Update all 3 internal call sites in `hsp_selftest` to pass the exact buffer sizes (256u32 each). Extend corpus 384 to add a direct negative-path KAT: extern `hsp_predict_quorum` explicitly, call it with `out_cap = 159u32` (< 6*32=192 for the n=7,qsize=6 case), assert return == HSP_E_OVERFLOW; then call with `out_cap = 192u32` (exactly sufficient), assert return == HSP_OK. This proves the guard fires on the bad-cap case and passes on the exact-fit case — not a dead path. The module header comment is updated to document the new parameter. No other module calls `hsp_predict_quorum` directly (grep confirms all callers are inside hotstuff_predict.iii itself).
callers_to_thread: C:\Users\Edwin Boston\OneDrive\Desktop\III\STDLIB\iii\aether\hotstuff_predict.iii:hsp_selftest (lines 86, 92, 97 — 3 call sites, all updated to pass 256u32)
kat:
Corpus test 384_hotstuff_predict.iii extended with two new arms:

NEGATIVE (proves guard fires): hsp_init with n=7 (f=2, qsize=6), then hsp_predict_quorum(view=3, out_cap=159). Expected: return == -3 (HSP_E_OVERFLOW). 159 < 192 (= 6*32), so the check `qsize > out_cap / 32u32` evaluates `6u32 > 159u32 / 32u32 = 4u32` => `6 > 4` => true => overflow returned. A cap of 159 is chosen to be provably below the 192-byte threshold while being large enough that a naive n*32 or f*32 calculation would not accidentally protect it.

POSITIVE (proves exact-fit succeeds): same init, hsp_predict_quorum(view=3, out_cap=192). Expected: return == 0 (HSP_OK), out_size == 6, out_quorum[0] == 4 (peer 3 key). Proves the guard does NOT fire when the buffer is exactly sufficient (boundary condition, not just a large buffer).

Existing assertions from hsp_selftest() (return 99 from main) remain as the baseline positive path.
